attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic at eight weeks' gestation requesting advice about her medications. She has a history of three episodes of postnatal depression following her previous pregnancies, each requiring hospitalisation. She has been well for the past two years on fluoxetine 40 mg daily. She does not take any other regular medications. She works as a teacher and lives with her partner and three children aged eight, six, and four years.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her mood is currently stable with no features of depression.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan regarding her antidepressant medication?
options: Gradually taper fluoxetine over four weeks
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a significant history of recurrent postnatal depression requiring hospitalisation, currently stable on fluoxetine therapy. The management decision needs to balance the risks of untreated maternal depression against potential risks of medication exposure to the developing fetus.

Continue fluoxetine at the current dose is the most appropriate option. Given her history of severe postnatal depression requiring hospitalisation, she is at high risk of relapse if medication is discontinued. Fluoxetine has extensive safety data in pregnancy and is considered one of the preferred antidepressants during pregnancy. The risks of untreated depression (poor nutrition, substance use, suicide) generally outweigh the small risks associated with fluoxetine use.

Cease fluoxetine immediately would place this patient at high risk of relapse given her history of severe depression. Abrupt discontinuation could also lead to discontinuation symptoms. The risks of untreated depression during pregnancy are significant for both mother and baby.

Gradually taper fluoxetine still carries the risk of relapse and is not necessary given that fluoxetine is considered safe in pregnancy. The current stability of her mental health is valuable and should be maintained.

Switch to sertraline is unnecessary as fluoxetine is already a suitable choice for pregnancy. Changing medications carries risks of destabilising her current well-controlled mental health without providing any clear benefit.

Psychological therapy alone would be insufficient given her history of severe depression requiring hospitalisation. While psychological support is valuable, it should be provided in addition to, not instead of, medication in this high-risk scenario.

The key principle is that untreated depression during pregnancy carries significant risks, and maintaining stability with a medication known to be relatively safe in pregnancy is often the best approach. Close monitoring throughout pregnancy and the postpartum period is essential.
learningPoints: Untreated antenatal depression carries significant risks for both mother and baby including poor nutrition, substance use, and suicide.
Continuing antidepressants in pregnancy, when benefits outweigh risks, is often safer than discontinuing treatment.
Fluoxetine is considered one of the safer antidepressants during pregnancy with extensive safety data.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Management of women with mental health issues during pregnancy and the postnatal period: good practice guide no. 14 [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2011 [cited 2024 Jun 23]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/media/4gikqggv/managementwomenmentalhealthgoodpractice14.pdf

Horsfall J. Antidepressants in pregnancy and breastfeeding [Internet]. Aust Prescr. 2018 [cited 2024 Jun 23];41(1):10-3. Available from: https://australianprescriber.tg.org.au/articles/antidepressants-in-pregnancy-and-breastfeeding.html

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
